course_info.title: Advanced Programming Concepts
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Understand pure functions, immutability, and referential transparency
Apply higher-order functions: map, filter, reduce
Write programs using functional composition and pipelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Most professional code mixes paradigms. This course expands your programming toolkit beyond OOP to include functional, concurrent, and generic programming. Each paradigm offers different strengths for different problems.
key_concepts: Pure functions: same input always produces same output, no side effects
Immutability: data cannot be changed after creation, new values are derived
Higher-order functions: functions that take or return functions
Function composition: building complex operations from simple ones
Closures: functions that capture their lexical environment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rewrite an imperative data processing pipeline using functional style: replace loops with map/filter/reduce, replace mutation with immutable transformations, and compare readability and testability

review.value: An expression is referentially transparent if it can be replaced with its value without changing program behavior; this enables equational reasoning, memoization, and safe parallelization
Immutable data cannot have race conditions because no thread can modify it; this eliminates the need for locks, making concurrent programming dramatically simpler and safer

learning_objectives: Implement concurrent programs using async/await and message passing
Understand the actor model and CSP (Communicating Sequential Processes)
readings: Goetz, Java Concurrency in Practice, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Concurrency is about dealing with multiple things at once; parallelism is about doing multiple things at once. Modern software must handle both: responsive UIs, network I/O, and multi-core computation all require concurrent thinking.
key_concepts: Concurrency vs parallelism: structure vs execution
async/await: cooperative multitasking for I/O-bound work
Message passing: goroutines and channels (CSP model)
Actor model: isolated state with asynchronous message passing
Thread pools: managing thread lifecycle for CPU-bound parallelism
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a web scraper three ways: sequential, async/await (I/O concurrency), and thread pool (CPU parallelism for parsing), comparing throughput and code complexity

review.value: Instead of communicating by sharing memory (with locks), share memory by communicating (with channels); this is the Go proverb that captures the CSP approach to safe concurrent programming

async/await uses a single thread with cooperative scheduling (no context switch overhead, limited to I/O-bound work); threads use OS scheduling (real parallelism, higher overhead, requires synchronization for shared state)

learning_objectives: Understand static vs dynamic typing and type inference
Implement generic/parameterized types for reusable code
Use type constraints and bounded polymorphism
readings: Pierce, Types and Programming Languages, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Type systems are a form of lightweight formal verification: they catch entire classes of bugs at compile time. Generic programming lets you write code that works with any type that satisfies specified constraints, maximizing reuse without sacrificing type safety.
key_concepts: Static typing: types checked at compile time (Java, Rust, TypeScript)
Dynamic typing: types checked at runtime (Python, JavaScript)
Type inference: compiler deduces types from usage (Rust, TypeScript, Kotlin)
Generics: parameterized types and functions (List<T>, fn sort<T: Ord>)
Traits and interfaces: defining behavior contracts for generic bounds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a generic priority queue in Rust that works with any type implementing the Ord trait, then contrast with a Python version that accepts any type but fails at runtime if comparison is unsupported
setup_requirements: TypeScript environment for type system examples

review.value: Java generics use type erasure (generic types become Object at runtime); Rust generics use monomorphization (compiler generates specialized code for each concrete type). Monomorphization gives zero-cost abstraction but increases binary size

Sum types (enums with data) and product types (structs/tuples) together form algebraic data types; they enable exhaustive pattern matching, making it impossible to forget a case when handling variants

learning_objectives: Use decorators, macros, and reflection for code generation
Evaluate when metaprogramming helps vs when it obscures
readings: Lopes, Exercises in Programming Style, selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Metaprogramming is writing code that writes or modifies code. Used judiciously, it eliminates boilerplate and enforces invariants. Used carelessly, it creates unmaintainable magic. This session teaches you when and how to use it responsibly.
key_concepts: Python decorators: wrapping functions to add behavior
Python metaclasses: customizing class creation
Rust procedural macros: compile-time code generation
Reflection: inspecting types and values at runtime
Tradeoffs: power vs readability, magic vs explicitness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Python decorator that automatically validates function arguments against type annotations, a retry decorator for network calls, and a Rust derive macro that generates serialization code

review.value: Decorators wrap a function to add behavior (logging, caching, authentication) without modifying the function's code; they are the most common and most readable form of metaprogramming in Python

If a simple function or class achieves the same goal, prefer it; metaprogramming should be a last resort for eliminating genuine repetitive boilerplate, not a first choice for showing off language features

learning_objectives: Understand garbage collection, reference counting, and ownership models
Profile programs to identify performance bottlenecks
Apply optimization techniques guided by profiling data
readings: Godbolt, 'What Every Programmer Should Know About Memory'

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance matters. Understanding how memory is managed in different languages — and how to measure and optimize performance — separates programmers who build fast software from those who wonder why their code is slow.
key_concepts: Stack vs heap allocation: speed vs flexibility
Garbage collection: mark-and-sweep, generational GC, GC pauses
Reference counting: deterministic deallocation, circular reference problem
Rust ownership: compile-time memory management with no GC overhead
Profiling: CPU profiling, memory profiling, flame graphs, benchmarking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Profile a Python program to find a performance bottleneck, optimize it in Python, then rewrite the hot path in Rust (via PyO3) to achieve a 50x speedup, measuring before and after

review.value: Each value has exactly one owner; when the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. Borrowing allows temporary read-only or exclusive mutable access. This system prevents use-after-free, double-free, and data races at compile time with zero runtime cost

Never guess where bottlenecks are; always measure first. Programmers' intuition about performance is consistently wrong. Profile, identify the hot path, optimize that path, and measure again to verify improvement

learning_objectives: Apply design patterns from OOP, functional, and concurrent paradigms
Recognize when a pattern from one paradigm solves a problem in another
Build programs that combine paradigms effectively
readings: Gamma et al., Design Patterns (selected: Strategy, Observer, Iterator)

lecture_notes.introduction: Design patterns are not language-specific; they are solutions to recurring problems. Many OOP patterns (Strategy, Observer, Iterator) have simpler functional equivalents. Understanding patterns across paradigms lets you choose the simplest solution.
key_concepts: Strategy pattern: OOP (interface + classes) vs functional (pass a function)
Observer pattern: OOP (callbacks) vs reactive (streams and subscribers)
Iterator pattern: OOP (interface) vs generators and lazy sequences
Builder pattern: OOP (method chaining) vs functional (pipeline composition)
Choosing the right paradigm for the problem at hand
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a configurable data pipeline using Strategy pattern in OOP style, then refactor to functional style using higher-order functions, comparing verbosity and flexibility

review.value: Peter Norvig observed that many Gang of Four patterns are workarounds for language limitations; first-class functions eliminate Strategy, closures eliminate Command, and algebraic data types eliminate Visitor. Choose languages that make your patterns unnecessary

Prefer composing behaviors from small, focused components over deep inheritance hierarchies; composition is more flexible (swap behaviors at runtime), more testable (mock individual components), and avoids the fragile base class problem

questions.answer:
from functools import reduce
result = reduce(lambda acc, x: acc + x, map(lambda n: n * n, filter(lambda n: n % 2 == 0, numbers)))

# Or more readably:
evens = filter(lambda n: n % 2 == 0, numbers)
squared = map(lambda n: n * n, evens)
result = reduce(lambda acc, x: acc + x, squared)

# Result: 4 + 16 + 36 + 64 + 100 = 220

rubric.key: Correct use of filter for even numbers
Correct use of map for squaring
Correct use of reduce for summation

questions.question: Explain the difference between concurrency and parallelism with a real-world analogy and a programming example of each.
questions.answer: Concurrency is about managing multiple tasks that can overlap in time (structure). Parallelism is about executing multiple tasks simultaneously (execution). Analogy: A single cook preparing two dishes by switching between them is concurrent but not parallel. Two cooks each preparing one dish is parallel. Programming: async/await handling multiple HTTP requests on one thread is concurrent (switches between requests during I/O waits). A thread pool processing images on 4 CPU cores is parallel (truly simultaneous computation).

questions.question: Write a generic function in Rust (or pseudocode with type constraints) that finds the maximum element in a slice. Explain what trait bound is required and why.

rubric.key: Correct generic function with trait bound
Explains why the trait bound is necessary

questions.question: Write a Python decorator called @retry(max_attempts=3, delay=1) that retries a function up to max_attempts times if it raises an exception, waiting delay seconds between attempts.

rubric.key: Correct three-level nesting for parameterized decorator
Correct retry loop with attempt counting
Re-raises last exception after all attempts fail

major_assignments.title: Multi-Paradigm Programming Project
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a concurrent data processing system that combines functional, object-oriented, and concurrent programming paradigms. The system must process a stream of events using a configurable pipeline of transformation and aggregation stages, with concurrent execution for I/O and computation.
objectives: Apply functional programming for data transformation stages
Use OOP for system structure and configuration
Implement concurrency for parallel processing and I/O
Profile and optimize the system based on measured bottlenecks
deliverables: Source code implementing the multi-paradigm pipeline
Benchmark results comparing sequential vs concurrent execution
Profiling report identifying and addressing bottlenecks
Design document (3 pages) explaining paradigm choices for each component

criteria: Transformation stages are pure functions with no side effects
Pipeline is composable: stages can be added, removed, or reordered
Higher-order functions used appropriately for configuration
Immutable data flows between stages
I/O-bound work uses async/await or equivalent
CPU-bound work uses thread/process parallelism
No race conditions or deadlocks
Graceful error handling in concurrent contexts
Clear separation between paradigms with justified boundaries
OOP used for system structure, FP for data processing, concurrency for execution
Code is modular and each module is independently testable
Configuration is data-driven, not hardcoded
Profiling identifies actual bottlenecks with data
Concurrent version shows measurable speedup over sequential
Design document explains paradigm choices with rationale
Performance analysis is honest about trade-offs
Effective combination of functional, OOP, and concurrent paradigms
Correct concurrent execution with measured performance improvement
Clean architecture with justified paradigm boundaries
Thorough profiling and honest performance analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of advanced programming concepts

lab_assignments.description: Weekly exercises in each programming paradigm

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam on functional and concurrent programming

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all paradigms and patterns

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for lectures and mandatory for lab sessions.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of paradigm concepts is encouraged. All code and analysis must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own. Using code from external sources without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+, Rust toolchain, and Go installed. Lab computers are available with all environments configured.
